title: Graph-Based Visualisation of Username-Linked Data Exposure
summary: Designed and developed an ethically constrained proof-of-concept that explores how publicly available usernames can be used to infer relationships between social media platforms, data brokers and wider online identity through interactive graph visualisation.
context: Modern digital identities are distributed across hundreds of online services, many of which expose fragments of publicly accessible information. This research investigates how those fragments can be aggregated to better understand digital exposure while deliberately avoiding deterministic identification or encouraging misuse.
challenge: Design a research platform capable of visualising username-linked relationships across online services while balancing technical capability with ethical responsibility, privacy-by-design principles and misuse prevention.
approach: Designed a modular Java application with a lightweight web interface, separating platform definitions from application logic to support extensibility. Implemented confidence-based inference rather than deterministic confirmation, applied constrained output techniques to reduce misuse potential and represented inferred relationships using an interactive knowledge graph.
outcome: Produced a complete MSc dissertation supported by a functional proof-of-concept application, literature review, software architecture, ethical framework and evaluation of graph-based approaches to digital identity visualisation.

impact.whyItMattered: The project explores how publicly available information can be aggregated to reveal broader patterns of digital exposure, encouraging greater awareness of online privacy while demonstrating how technical solutions can be designed to minimise misuse.
publicValue: Promotes greater understanding of digital identity exposure
Encourages responsible awareness of online privacy risks
Demonstrates ethical approaches to OSINT research
Supports discussion around responsible software engineering and privacy-by-design
organisationalValue: Demonstrates the application of secure software engineering principles
Balances technical capability with governance and ethical considerations
Illustrates modular software architecture for future extensibility
Provides a reusable framework for visualising interconnected data relationships

lessons: Engineering capability should always be accompanied by ethical responsibility. Designing systems that are transparent, privacy-conscious and resistant to misuse is as important as solving the technical problem itself.
